title: Health endpoint
description: Expose GET /api/health that returns 200 OK with JSON containing status, build version, and timestamp. This is the first vertical slice: it validates the harness, the Spring Boot setup, and the verification pipeline.
acceptance: GET /api/health returns 200 with a JSON body containing 'status', 'version', and 'timestamp'.
A controller test asserts the contract.
./init.sh passes.

title: Core domain models
description: Define the core domain types: Player, Room, Game, Move, GameStatus, Side. Use records for value objects and entities for things that will be persisted later. No persistence yet — these are in-memory types only.
acceptance: Records and enums exist under io.github.dariogguillen.chess.domain.
Unit tests cover invariants (e.g., a Room cannot have more than 2 players).
./init.sh passes.

title: Server-side move validation
description: Integrate the chesslib library. Provide a ChessRules service that, given a current FEN and a proposed move (from-square, to-square, optional promotion), returns the new FEN, whether the move was legal, and the resulting GameStatus (ongoing, check, checkmate, stalemate, draw).
acceptance: ChessRules service has a clear API and JavaDoc.
Tests cover: legal move, illegal move, check, checkmate, stalemate, castling, en passant, promotion.
./init.sh passes.

title: Room REST endpoints (in-memory)
description: REST endpoints to create a room and join a room. State lives in an in-memory store for now (a thread-safe Map). Maps roughly to the Node createRoom and joinRoom events. WebSocket comes later.
acceptance: POST /api/rooms creates a room and returns the room ID.
POST /api/rooms/{id}/join joins an existing room as the second player.
Errors return appropriate HTTP status with a structured error body (room not found, room full, etc.).
Integration tests cover happy path and error cases.
./init.sh passes.

title: OpenAPI spec and Swagger UI via springdoc
description: Adopt springdoc-openapi-starter-webmvc-ui to auto-generate an OpenAPI 3 spec from the existing REST endpoints and mount Swagger UI for interactive exploration. Enrich the spec with @Operation summaries and @ApiResponse declarations so every 2xx and 4xx response is documented honestly, with ErrorResponse as the body schema for error cases. Adopted now (between room-rest-api and game-rest-api) so each subsequent REST feature ships its endpoints with springdoc annotations from day one rather than accumulating retroactive doc churn. WebSocket/STOMP is intentionally out of scope; that surface is documented in the README when feature 6 lands.
acceptance: springdoc-openapi-starter-webmvc-ui is added to pom.xml.
GET /v3/api-docs returns a valid OpenAPI 3 JSON spec covering /api/health, POST /api/rooms, and POST /api/rooms/{id}/join.
Swagger UI is reachable at /swagger-ui.html and renders the three endpoints with their parameters, request/response schemas, and error responses.
Each endpoint declares its 2xx and the relevant 4xx responses via @ApiResponse, with ErrorResponse as the body schema for 4xx.
README documents the Swagger UI URL and /v3/api-docs for client codegen, and trims the manual endpoint table in favor of a link to the spec.
An integration test asserts that /v3/api-docs returns 200 with a payload containing each documented operation and that /swagger-ui.html returns a 200 HTML response.
./init.sh passes.

title: Game REST endpoints (in-memory)
description: REST endpoints to make a move and to query game state. Move requests are validated by ChessRules. Game state lives in the in-memory store.
acceptance: POST /api/games/{id}/moves submits a move; rejects illegal moves with 422.
GET /api/games/{id} returns the current game state (FEN, turn, status, players).
Integration tests exercise a full short game (Fool's Mate or similar).
./init.sh passes.

title: Real-time updates over STOMP
description: Add Spring WebSocket with STOMP. After a move is accepted by the REST endpoint, the new state is broadcast to all subscribers of the game's topic. Subscribers include the opponent and any future spectators.
acceptance: WebSocket configured on /ws with STOMP.
After a successful move, opponents on /topic/games/{id} receive the new state.
Integration test connects two STOMP clients, makes a move, asserts the broadcast.
./init.sh passes.

title: Spectator mode with live viewer count
description: Allow non-player participants to subscribe to a game's STOMP topic and receive live state. The server tracks the current viewer count per game and publishes it so the UI can render it live. TENTATIVE: the exact approach — anonymous STOMP subscribers (no domain change) vs. domain-modeled viewers with identity — is decided during or after feature 6 (websocket-realtime), once the bus is in place and the trade-offs are concrete. The user explicitly wants the viewer count visible regardless of which path is taken.
acceptance: Non-player clients can subscribe to /topic/games/{id} and receive state updates.
Server tracks the current viewer count per game (via STOMP session tracking, domain modeling, or both).
Viewer count is published to subscribers (e.g. on /topic/games/{id}/viewers or as part of the state payload) so the UI can render it live.
Integration test: a viewer subscribes, sees a state update broadcast, the count ticks up; on viewer disconnect the count ticks down.
./init.sh passes.

title: Constrain ErrorResponse.error to the known error code enum
description: Annotate ErrorResponse.error with @Schema(allowableValues = {...}) enumerating the 9 error codes the GlobalExceptionHandler actually emits today (ROOM_NOT_FOUND, ROOM_FULL, GAME_NOT_FOUND, GAME_ALREADY_ENDED, ILLEGAL_MOVE, NOT_YOUR_TURN, VALIDATION_FAILED, MALFORMED_REQUEST, MISSING_HEADER). Springdoc renders this as an enum array in the OpenAPI spec; openapi-typescript on the frontend turns it into a TypeScript union literal automatically. Driven by cross-repo coordination with chess-frontend feature 3 (rest-room-integration). Includes an OpenApiIT canary asserting the enum array matches exactly, protecting against silent drift when a future feature adds a new error code without updating the @Schema.
acceptance: ErrorResponse.error annotated with @Schema(allowableValues = {...}) listing all 9 codes currently emitted by GlobalExceptionHandler.
@Schema description on the field explains its purpose and points to the exception hierarchy.
OpenApiIT extended (or new test) asserting components.schemas.ErrorResponse.properties.error.enum is an array of exactly the 9 expected codes, in a deterministic order.
Audit of GlobalExceptionHandler documented in the feature note: every handler path produces one of the 9 codes; no orphans, no missed codes.
docs/architecture.md API contract section lists the 9 codes with their HTTP status mappings (404/409/422/400).
./init.sh passes.

title: Containerize the backend (Dockerfile + local docker-compose)
description: Multi-stage Dockerfile (Eclipse Temurin JRE Jammy) producing a runnable image of the Spring Boot fat jar. docker-compose.yml for local development bringing up the app + Postgres + Redis as a self-contained stack. application.yml gains env-var-with-default configuration so the same artifact runs locally with Testcontainers, locally with docker-compose, and in production (set in features 7.5 and 7.7). README documents the three local-dev workflows. No CI/CD yet; that's feature 7.7.
acceptance: Dockerfile multi-stage: builder stage with Maven + JDK, runtime stage with Temurin JRE Jammy. Final image runs the Spring Boot fat jar.
Dockerfile includes HEALTHCHECK directive hitting /api/health.
.dockerignore excludes target/, .git/, .claude/, progress/, notes/, IDE files.

docker-compose.yml brings up app + Postgres 16 + Redis 7-alpine. depends_on with healthcheck conditions. Volumes for Postgres data persistence. Host port mappings: 8080 (app), 5432 (postgres), 6379 (redis).

application.yml uses ${SPRING_DATASOURCE_URL:fallback} env-var-with-default pattern for datasource (Postgres) and Redis. Local Testcontainers run still works (TestcontainersConfiguration sets the URLs via @ServiceConnection).
docker compose up --build starts the full stack; /api/health returns 200; /v3/api-docs returns valid OpenAPI 3 JSON.

README.md adds a 'Running with Docker' section documenting three local-dev workflows: (a) ./mvnw spring-boot:test-run with Testcontainers, (b) docker compose up, (c) hybrid (compose for infra + spring-boot:run for app).
docs/architecture.md mentions the container layer briefly as the deployment artifact shape.
./init.sh passes unchanged (no Docker steps added to init.sh; verification of Docker is manual + by feature 7.7's CI smoke test).

title: Provision AWS infrastructure for the backend
description: Provision the AWS Free Tier infrastructure manually first, scripts after: EC2 t3.micro (Ubuntu 24.04), RDS Postgres db.t3.micro, Security Groups, Elastic IP. Set up Caddy as reverse proxy with auto-TLS via Let's Encrypt, pointing at a Duck DNS subdomain. Deploy the Docker image manually (pull from local for now; ECR auth comes in feature 7.7). Set up AWS Budget alarm at $1/month. Document everything in a runbook so the steps can be re-executed (and so feature 7.7's automation has clear targets). Includes Spring Boot's `server.forward-headers-strategy` so Springdoc reports the public HTTPS URL when running behind Caddy.
acceptance: EC2 t3.micro running Ubuntu 24.04 in us-east-2, with Docker installed and a non-root user for app operations.
RDS Postgres db.t3.micro in the same region, security group allowing only the EC2 instance's IP.
Elastic IP attached to the EC2 instance.
Duck DNS subdomain (e.g. chess-backend.duckdns.org) pointing at the Elastic IP.
Caddy reverse-proxy on the EC2 instance terminating TLS via Let's Encrypt for the Duck DNS subdomain, proxying to the backend container on port 8080.
Redis self-hosted in a Docker container on the same EC2 (no ElastiCache; Redis is not in AWS free tier).
Backend container running, healthcheck green, /api/health reachable over HTTPS at the public subdomain.
AWS Budget alarm at $1/month with email notification.
ECR repository created (lifecycle policy: keep last 5 images). No push automation yet (that's 7.7).
Runbook in docs/ (or notes/) capturing every manual step taken, with the exact AWS console paths or CLI invocations, so the steps can be replayed or migrated.

`/v3/api-docs` reports the public HTTPS URL as `servers[0].url` when the app receives `X-Forwarded-Proto: https` (Caddy default), so Swagger UI's `Try it out` works without mixed-content errors. Regression-covered by an IT.

title: GitHub Actions CI/CD pipeline with OIDC and ECR
description: Automate the deploy. GitHub Actions workflow runs ./init.sh, builds the Docker image, pushes to ECR using OIDC-federated authentication (no long-lived AWS access keys), SSHes into the EC2 instance, and triggers a docker pull + container restart. Includes a post-deploy smoke test against the production /api/health endpoint. Idempotent: pushing the same commit twice is safe.
acceptance: .github/workflows/deploy.yml triggers on push to main.
Job runs ./init.sh; build fails if init.sh red.
AWS OIDC trust configured: IAM identity provider for token.actions.githubusercontent.com, IAM role with trust policy for the repo, scoped permissions (ECR push + minimal else).
GitHub Actions uses aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials with role-to-assume; no AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID secret stored.
Docker image tagged with both the commit SHA and 'latest'; pushed to ECR.
SSH step uses a deploy-specific key (stored in GitHub Actions secret), pulls the new image on EC2, restarts the container via docker compose pull + up -d.
Post-deploy smoke test: curl https://<duckdns-subdomain>/api/health; fail the workflow on non-200.
Concurrency group set so two pushes to main don't deploy in parallel.
README documents the deploy flow with a high-level diagram or sequence.
Feature 12 (github-actions-ci) is re-scoped to PR-only validation workflow (this feature covered the deploy automation).

title: Active game state in Redis
description: Move the in-memory state of active games and rooms to Redis. Active rooms get a TTL (e.g., 24h) so abandoned rooms self-clean. Spring Data Redis with a synchronous template.
acceptance: Rooms and active games are stored in Redis, keyed by room/game ID.
TTL is applied and refreshed on activity.
Existing REST and WebSocket tests still pass, now against Testcontainers-Redis.
./init.sh passes.

title: Game history in Postgres
description: Persist completed games to Postgres for history. JPA entities, Flyway migrations for the schema. Endpoint to list a player's recent games.
acceptance: Flyway migration V1__games.sql creates the games table.
Completed games are saved with players, result, move history.
GET /api/players/{id}/games returns the player's history.
Integration test against Testcontainers-Postgres.
./init.sh passes.

title: Room state read + real-time lifecycle events
description: Fix a critical functional gap: the room creator (Player A) currently has no way to learn when Player B joins and the game starts. The REST POST /api/rooms returns gameId: null (intentionally, the game does not exist yet); POST /api/rooms/{id}/join returns gameId only to Player B; Player A never receives it and cannot subscribe to /topic/games/{gameId}. The game cannot be played end-to-end through the public API today. Frontend repo's progress/current.md confirms this is the blocker for the creator-game-discovery flow. Fix in two complementary parts: (1) NEW REST endpoint GET /api/rooms/{id} returning room state including gameId (or null). Required by the frontend even with STOMP in place, because STOMP late-subscribers cannot replay (fallback path, and the primary mechanism if frontend chooses polling). (2) NEW STOMP topic /topic/rooms/{roomId} carrying lifecycle events with a 'type' discriminator field. Today: RoomJoinedEvent. Future: RoomClosedEvent, PlayerLeftEvent (out of scope here but topic shape supports them). Player A subscribes immediately after createRoom, receives RoomJoinedEvent on B's join, transitions to /topic/games/{gameId}. Cross-repo coordination with chess-frontend repo's pending creator-game-discovery feature.
acceptance: NEW REST endpoint GET /api/rooms/{id} returning 200 with a RoomDetailsResponse { roomId (String, 6-char short code), players (array of { id: UUID, displayName: String, role: 'WHITE' | 'BLACK' }), gameId (UUID, nullable), status (one of 'WAITING_FOR_PLAYER' | 'ACTIVE' | 'CLOSED' — backend native names) }, or 404 ROOM_NOT_FOUND if the room does not exist.

Role is derived from join order: players[0] is WHITE (the creator), players[1] (when present) is BLACK (the joiner). The Room domain record is unchanged — role inference lives in the controller/mapper layer.
Endpoint is idempotent, no body, no path-level auth; matches the existing security posture (room id is the shared secret).
Springdoc: @Tag, @Operation, @ApiResponse 200 (with RoomDetailsResponse schema), @ApiResponse 404 (with ErrorResponse schema). RoomDetailsResponse fields carry @Schema where non-obvious.
NEW STOMP topic /topic/rooms/{roomId} (no WebSocketConfig change — /topic is already registered).
A sealed interface RoomEvent permits RoomJoinedEvent with String type() and String roomId() exposes a forward-compat discriminator.
RoomJoinedEvent record carries type ('ROOM_JOINED' constant), roomId (String, 6-char short code), gameId (UUID), and blackPlayer (Player record with id UUID + displayName).

RoomService.joinRoom broadcasts RoomJoinedEvent on /topic/rooms/{roomId} via SimpMessagingTemplate after the game is created and both stores (Redis room + Redis game) are persisted, in a try/catch + WARN log mirroring GameService.broadcastMoveEvent.

GET endpoint IT: 200 with the expected payload across both phases (WAITING_FOR_PLAYER with gameId=null and one WHITE player; ACTIVE with gameId set and both players with WHITE/BLACK roles); 404 for unknown room id.
STOMP IT: client subscribed BEFORE a second client joins receives RoomJoinedEvent with type='ROOM_JOINED', matching roomId/gameId/blackPlayer.
STOMP IT: client subscribing AFTER join does NOT receive the event (locks in no-replay semantics).
STOMP IT: duplicate join to a full room (409 ROOM_FULL) does NOT broadcast a second event.
STOMP IT: wire-format roundtrip — the JSON payload contains a 'type' field with value 'ROOM_JOINED' (locks in the discriminator).
docs/architecture.md: STOMP contract section updated with the new topic and discriminator pattern; API contract section updated with GET /api/rooms/{id} shape.

Feature note notes/09.5-room-lifecycle-events.md follows the template; Scala/Typelevel parallels include Topic[F, Event] (fs2) and sealed ADT + circe withDiscriminator analogies; documents why we did NOT retrofit MoveEvent / ViewerCountEvent with a type field (single-event-type topics).
./init.sh passes.

title: CORS configuration for REST endpoints
description: Add a CORS configuration so the deployed frontend on GitHub Pages can call /api/** in the browser. Backend currently has CORS only for the WebSocket handshake (WebSocketConfig#setAllowedOriginPatterns). The REST surface is silent, which means the browser blocks the preflight OPTIONS /api/rooms for any cross-origin request. Unblocks chess-frontend production E2E (its progress/current.md lists this as cross-repo item #1). The allowed origins mirror the WebSocket list (https://dariogguillen.github.io for prod GitHub Pages, http://localhost:* for dev). Implementation via a WebMvcConfigurer (NOT @CrossOrigin per controller — single source of truth for origin policy). Extracts the origin list to @ConfigurationProperties so REST and WebSocket share the same source of truth (avoids the drift the previous two-copy approach would invite). Includes ITs asserting the preflight succeeds for an allowed origin and is rejected for a disallowed one (drift canary).
acceptance: A WebMvcConfigurer bean registers CORS mappings for /api/** with allowed origins matching the WebSocket configuration (https://dariogguillen.github.io and http://localhost:* via setAllowedOriginPatterns).
Allowed methods cover GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, OPTIONS.
Allowed headers cover Content-Type and Accept.
allowCredentials is set explicitly with the rationale documented in the feature note (default to false since the API is stateless JSON; flip to true only when auth ships).
Preflight IT: OPTIONS /api/rooms with Origin: https://dariogguillen.github.io returns 200 with the expected Access-Control-Allow-* headers.
Drift canary IT: OPTIONS /api/rooms with Origin: https://evil.example returns 403 (or omits the Access-Control-Allow-Origin header) so a future origin-list edit cannot accidentally widen the surface.
Actual cross-origin POST IT: a request with the allowed origin succeeds; one with a disallowed origin fails the CORS check.

docs/architecture.md updated: the existing 'Allowed origins (CORS for the WebSocket handshake)' subsection becomes a single 'CORS' section covering REST + WebSocket, with the obsolete 'mirrors the existing CORS strategy on the REST side' line replaced by an accurate description.
./init.sh passes.

title: Disconnect and reconnect (core)
description: Core lifecycle: track player STOMP session state, start a 60s grace period on disconnect, clear the timer and restore the game on reconnect, or mark the game ABANDONED + archive it to Postgres on timeout. The opponent receives a minimal terminal STOMP broadcast so they know the outcome. Mid-grace UX countdown (per-second updates for the opponent's UI) is deliberately split into feature 11.5 (disconnect-notifications) to keep this feature focused on the lifecycle correctness rather than the live UX polish.
acceptance: STOMP player identification: the existing 'playerId' header pattern (ViewerCountTracker) is reused to associate a STOMP session with a player UUID.
Track only the 2 players of a game; spectators are explicitly out of scope for this lifecycle (they remain handled by ViewerCountTracker).
On SessionDisconnectEvent for a player whose game is still non-terminal, start a per-player grace-period timer of 60s (configurable via @ConfigurationProperties).
If the same playerId resubscribes to the same game within 60s, the timer is cancelled and the game continues unchanged.

If the timer fires, the game is mutated in Redis to status=ABANDONED via gameStore.compute, the GameHistoryService.archive path is invoked (the archive trigger today lives in GameService.applyMove; this feature adds a second invocation site or refactors to a shared helper), and a terminal STOMP event is broadcast to /topic/games/{gameId} so the opponent learns the outcome.

Idempotency: if a disconnect handler fires for a game already in a terminal status (e.g., the game ended by checkmate while the player happened to disconnect), the timer is a no-op — no second archive, no second broadcast.

Server restart edge case is acknowledged in the note as a documented limitation (in-flight timers are lost; the game stays in ONGOING until something else terminates it). Out of scope for this feature.

Both-players-disconnect edge case: the first player whose timer fires causes the abandonment; the second timer fires on an already-terminal game and is a no-op (covered by the idempotency rule above).

Integration tests cover: (a) connect → disconnect → wait 60s → assert ABANDONED + archived to Postgres + terminal STOMP event delivered; (b) connect → disconnect → reconnect within grace → assert game still ONGOING and no archive; (c) disconnect-on-already-terminal-game → assert no-op.

Scheduling infrastructure added: @EnableScheduling on ChessApplication (or a new @Configuration) + a TaskScheduler bean. Document why the precise per-disconnect scheduling approach was chosen over polling @Scheduled, in the feature note.
Clock-controlled tests: the new components inject the existing Clock bean; tests can use a fixed/mock Clock to assert grace-period timing without sleeping for 60s.
./init.sh passes.

title: Live disconnect/reconnect notifications (polish)
description: UX polish on top of feature 11 (disconnect-handling core). When a player disconnects, the opponent should see in their UI 'Bob is reconnecting, 45s remaining' with a countdown, and an explicit notification when the player reconnects. This feature adds the mid-grace STOMP broadcasts (PlayerDisconnectedEvent + PlayerReconnectedEvent) and retrofits the /topic/games/{gameId} topic to a sealed-interface GameStateEvent family with a type discriminator field, mirroring the pattern feature 9.5 introduced on /topic/rooms/{roomId}. Cross-repo coordination: the frontend will need to switch from shape-based discrimination to type-based.
acceptance: Sealed interface GameStateEvent permits the existing terminal events + new PlayerDisconnectedEvent + PlayerReconnectedEvent.

On disconnect: broadcast PlayerDisconnectedEvent { type, gameId, playerId, secondsRemaining } to /topic/games/{gameId}. Either a single event with the full grace period (frontend counts down locally) or per-second tick events (server is the source of truth). Decision documented in the plan.
On reconnect within grace: broadcast PlayerReconnectedEvent { type, gameId, playerId } so the opponent's UI clears the countdown banner.
Existing MoveEvent retrofitted into the sealed interface with a type field (the polymorphic-topic-gets-discriminator rule from feature 9.5). Frontend coordination required.
Integration tests cover the new broadcasts.
Feature note documents the decision (per-second tick vs single event with countdown on client) with the trade-off.
./init.sh passes.

title: Allow X-Player-Id header in REST CORS
description: Bug cazado durante testing E2E con el frontend: feature 10's CorsConfig allowedHeaders is currently 'Content-Type, Accept', but GameController.applyMove (POST /api/games/{id}/moves) requires the X-Player-Id native header (line 128: @RequestHeader("X-Player-Id") UUID playerId). The browser blocks the preflight when the frontend sends this header from a cross-origin context. Only affects production direct CORS (dev with Vite proxy bypasses CORS). Trivial fix: add X-Player-Id to allowedHeaders + a CorsConfigIT test asserting the header survives the preflight Access-Control-Allow-Headers list.
acceptance: CorsConfig.addCorsMappings allowedHeaders includes 'X-Player-Id' alongside 'Content-Type' and 'Accept'.
JavaDoc on CorsConfig updated: the dead-config rationale on the Authorization header stays; the X-Player-Id rationale links to POST /api/games/{id}/moves.
New IT in CorsConfigIT: OPTIONS /api/games/{id}/moves with Access-Control-Request-Headers: X-Player-Id and allowed Origin returns 200 with X-Player-Id in Access-Control-Allow-Headers.
Existing CorsConfigIT tests stay green (the existing preflight tests asked for Content-Type only; they keep working).

Feature note notes/11.7-cors-x-player-id.md documents the bug discovery (cross-repo E2E test, X-Player-Id introduced in feature 5 game-rest-api without updating the CORS allow-list added later in feature 10).
./init.sh passes.

title: INFO logs on successful STOMP broadcasts
description: The diagnosis of feature 11.7's reported broadcast bug (RoomJoinedEvent not reaching subscribers) required enabling TRACE-level logging on org.springframework.web.socket + .messaging + .messaging.simp.broker — that diagnosis would have been trivially confirmable from the production-level logs if every messagingTemplate.convertAndSend(...) site logged its successful broadcast. Today only failures log (WARN); successes are silent. This feature adds a single INFO line after each of the six broadcast points (RoomService.broadcastRoomJoinedEvent, GameService.broadcastMoveEvent, GameAbandonService.abandon, PlayerSessionTracker.broadcastDisconnected, PlayerSessionTracker.broadcastReconnected, ViewerCountTracker.broadcast) so a future operator (or the user diagnosing a similar issue) can confirm 'broadcast happened, broker took it' from INFO logs alone. Operational logging, not a behavioural change. The lesson — observability gap surfaced by a real cross-repo bug investigation — is documented in the feature note as a case study.
acceptance: Each of the six broadcast sites emits an INFO log AFTER the messagingTemplate.convertAndSend call returns successfully. The log carries the destination and the key identifier of the payload (roomId / gameId / playerId as applicable) but NOT the full payload.
Existing WARN-on-RuntimeException log lines stay; they cover the failure path.
Class-level JavaDoc updates on the six modified main classes briefly mention the new INFO log line (one-sentence cross-reference, not a rewrite).
No new tests — operational logs are not behavioural. All 181 existing tests stay green WITHOUT MODIFICATION on disk; the regression signal is that nothing else changed.

Feature note notes/11.8-broadcast-observability.md documents the bug-driven motivation (the feature 11.7 / bug #3 investigation), the INFO-vs-DEBUG and after-vs-before choices, why we did NOT extract a shared helper, and why no tests are added.
./init.sh passes.

title: Docker Compose for local development
description: Docker Compose file that spins up the app, Postgres, and Redis. Document the workflow in README.
acceptance: docker compose up -d brings up app + postgres + redis on local ports.
README has a 'Run locally' section.
./init.sh passes.

title: CI pipeline on GitHub Actions (PR validation)
description: GitHub Actions workflow that runs ./init.sh on every pull request (not on push to main — feature 7.7's deploy.yml already validates that path). Catches regressions before they hit main. Caches Maven dependencies. Adds a status badge to README.
acceptance: .github/workflows/ci.yml exists and runs ./init.sh on pull_request events targeting main.
Maven cache configured (actions/setup-java@v4 with cache: maven).
Workflow uses concurrency group keyed by PR number with cancel-in-progress: true (a new push to the PR cancels the in-flight check).
Workflow runs on ubuntu-latest, Java 21 Temurin (mirroring deploy.yml).
PR is blocked from merge if the workflow fails (user adds branch protection via GitHub UI; out of code scope but documented).
Status badge added to README near the top.
./init.sh passes locally and remotely.

title: Polished README with architecture diagram
description: Final README with: project overview, stack, how to run, architecture diagram (Mermaid), API summary, screenshots if available. This is the artifact a recruiter will read.
acceptance: README explains what the project is and how to run it in under 5 minutes.
Mermaid architecture diagram is rendered correctly on GitHub.
Links to harness engineering files (CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md) are present so reviewers can see the workflow.

title: Switch frontend CORS origin to Cloudflare Pages
description: The frontend migrated from GitHub Pages (https://dariogguillen.github.io) to Cloudflare Pages (https://chess-frontend-52i.pages.dev). The backend CORS allow-list, related tests, and docs need to swap origins. Pure config + docs change; no behavioural surface change beyond the allow-list itself. Reopens scope after the 2026-05-25 portfolio-closure milestone; precedent for treating a one-line CORS change as a full harness cycle was set by feature 11.7 (cors-x-player-id).
acceptance: application.yml default value for chess.cors.allowed-origin-patterns swaps https://dariogguillen.github.io for https://chess-frontend-52i.pages.dev; the localhost wildcard is kept.

CorsConfigIT GITHUB_PAGES_ORIGIN constant renamed to CLOUDFLARE_PAGES_ORIGIN with the new value; the three tests that use it (and the test-method name + class-JavaDoc bullet) are updated for consistency.
Drift canary preflight_disallowedOrigin_omitsCorsHeaders stays green — the policy stays restrictive.
docs/architecture.md CORS section updates the production-frontend bullet from GitHub Pages to Cloudflare Pages.
README.md Try-it-live frontend URL and description updated; the chess-frontend GitHub repo link (separate from the deploy URL) stays unchanged.
No stale 'dariogguillen.github.io' string survives anywhere in src/ or docs/ (excepting README.md's link to the GitHub repo itself, which is not a deploy URL).
./init.sh passes.

title: Authentication foundation (User entity, Spring Security base, JWT validation, /api/me)
description: First feature of the auth bundle (16–20). Introduces the User aggregate (uuid id, unique email, displayName, nullable passwordHash, nullable googleSub, createdAt), Flyway migration V2 for the users table, and two nullable FK columns on the existing games table (white_user_id, black_user_id) so future features can link authenticated users to their games on a fresh-start basis. NO intermediate players table — V1 explicitly denormalises player data onto games, and adding user FKs alongside the existing player snapshots is the cleanest fit with that shape. Also: Spring Security base config with a stateless filter chain that validates incoming JWTs (HS256, secret from env), and a single new endpoint GET /api/me that returns the authenticated user or 401. JWT issuance is NOT in scope (feature 17 owns it); this feature only adds the validator side so /api/me can be tested with a pre-generated token. The anonymous guest flow (POST /api/games, GET /api/games/{id}, STOMP /ws) stays open. CORS configuration is unchanged this feature (allowCredentials stays false; the Authorization header is already on the allow-list since feature 11.7's X-Player-Id work, but a new IT will lock the Bearer-header path).
acceptance: New domain User record/entity at io.github.dariogguillen.chess.domain.User with fields: UUID id, String email (unique), String displayName, String passwordHash (nullable), String googleSub (nullable), Instant createdAt.
New persistence UserRepository (Spring Data JPA) at io.github.dariogguillen.chess.persistence.UserRepository.

Flyway migration V2__create_users_and_game_user_links.sql creates the users table (id UUID PK; email TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE; display_name TEXT NOT NULL; password_hash TEXT NULL; google_sub TEXT NULL; created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL) with a partial unique index on google_sub WHERE google_sub IS NOT NULL. The same migration also alters the existing games table to add two nullable FK columns — white_user_id UUID NULL REFERENCES users(id) and black_user_id UUID NULL REFERENCES users(id) — each with a partial index WHERE the column IS NOT NULL. The migration does NOT create a players table; this is a deliberate departure from the first implementation pass — see progress/current.md bundle decision 6 for rationale.

New config SecurityConfig at io.github.dariogguillen.chess.config.SecurityConfig: stateless filter chain, BCryptPasswordEncoder bean, custom JwtAuthenticationFilter that reads Authorization: Bearer <token>, validates HS256 against auth.jwt.secret, loads the User by sub claim, and sets the SecurityContext.
Anonymous allow-list: POST /api/games, GET /api/games/{id}, /api/games/{id}/moves, /actuator/health, /v3/api-docs/**, /swagger-ui/**, /ws/**, OPTIONS * — all stay reachable without auth.

AuthProperties (@ConfigurationProperties prefix=auth.jwt) with fields: secret, expirySeconds (default 604800 = 7 days). Secret read from AUTH_JWT_SECRET env var with no default in prod yaml; test profile provides a fixed secret.
New web controller MeController at io.github.dariogguillen.chess.web.auth.MeController with GET /api/me returning 200 + MeResponse(id, email, displayName) when authenticated, 401 otherwise.

Integration test AuthCoreIT covers: (a) /api/me without Authorization header returns 401; (b) /api/me with a valid pre-generated JWT returns 200 with the expected user payload; (c) /api/me with an expired JWT returns 401; (d) /api/me with a malformed token returns 401.
Smoke IT or existing GameIT extension confirms anonymous game-create flow still returns 201 without any Authorization header.
docs/architecture.md gains an 'Authentication' section describing the User aggregate, stateless JWT model, fresh-start identity linking decision, and the bundle scope.
README.md NOT updated this feature — public API still anonymous-only until feature 17 lands the issuance endpoints. Reviewer confirms README claim is unchanged.
Feature note notes/16-auth-core.md follows _template.md with cross-ecosystem comparisons (Scala Typelevel parallels: pac4j / tsec / http4s middleware; jjwt-java vs the Scala equivalents).
./init.sh passes; test counts grow by the AuthCoreIT cases.

title: Email/password registration + login → JWT issuance
description: Second feature of the auth bundle. Adds POST /api/auth/register and POST /api/auth/login. Register validates email format + password strength (Jakarta Validation), creates a User with BCrypt password hash, and returns 201 with a fresh JWT. Login validates credentials and returns 200 with a JWT. Errors: 409 email taken, 400 validation, 401 bad credentials. The JWT shape (claims: sub=user.id, email, exp, iat) is locked here for the rest of the bundle.
acceptance: POST /api/auth/register accepts {email, password, displayName}, creates the User (BCrypt-hashed password), returns 201 + {token, user: {id, email, displayName}}.
POST /api/auth/login accepts {email, password}, returns 200 + {token, user} on success, 401 on bad credentials.
Validation errors return 400 with a structured error body matching the project's existing error format.
Duplicate email on register returns 409.
AuthService at io.github.dariogguillen.chess.service.auth.AuthService owns user creation, password verification, and JWT issuance via a new JwtIssuer service.
JwtIssuer/JwtVerifier symmetry: token issued in /api/auth/login is accepted by the JwtAuthenticationFilter from feature 16, and a subsequent GET /api/me with that token returns the same user.
Integration tests cover: register→login→/api/me round-trip, dup-email 409, bad-password 401, weak-password 400, malformed-email 400.
Feature note notes/17-auth-jwt.md.
README.md adds an 'Authentication (optional)' subsection in the API section pointing at the new endpoints; the Swagger UI is the canonical spec.
docs/architecture.md API-contract section gains the new endpoints with their request/response shapes.
./init.sh passes.

title: Google OAuth 2.0 sign-in → JWT redirect to frontend
description: Third feature of the auth bundle. Adds Spring Security OAuth2 client config with Google as the provider. On successful Google callback, the backend looks up the User by googleSub (or creates a new one with email + displayName lifted from the OAuth2User profile), then redirects the browser to https://chess-frontend-52i.pages.dev/auth/callback#token=<jwt> using the same JWT shape as feature 17. The frontend reads the fragment, stores the token, and clears it from the URL via history.replaceState (frontend work is out of this repo). Spring Security's default endpoints /oauth2/authorization/google and /login/oauth2/code/google are kept.
acceptance: OAuth2 client config in SecurityConfig (or a dedicated OAuth2ClientConfig) registers Google with client-id/client-secret read from GOOGLE_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID / GOOGLE_OAUTH_CLIENT_SECRET env vars (no defaults in prod yaml).

Custom AuthenticationSuccessHandler at io.github.dariogguillen.chess.config.OAuth2SuccessHandler: finds-or-creates the User by googleSub, issues a JWT via JwtIssuer (from feature 17), and redirects to the configured frontend callback URL with the token in the URL fragment.
auth.oauth.frontend-redirect-base property (env-overridable) holds the frontend's callback URL prefix; default is the Cloudflare Pages URL in prod, http://localhost:5173 in dev/test profiles.
CORS update: allowCredentials stays false because OAuth callback uses a top-level browser redirect (no XHR), so no CORS update is needed. Reviewer confirms.

Integration test OAuth2SuccessIT: stubs an OAuth2User in the SecurityContext (or uses Spring Security's testing support) and verifies the success handler creates the User and emits the redirect with the JWT fragment.
Integration test confirms /api/me works with the JWT minted by the OAuth flow (round-trip parity with feature 17's JWT).
Feature note notes/18-auth-google-oauth.md with the Google Cloud Console setup steps documented under 'Operator follow-ups' (creating the OAuth client, configuring the redirect URI).
docs/architecture.md Authentication section gains the OAuth flow diagram (Mermaid sequenceDiagram).
README.md mentions Google sign-in as an option.
./init.sh passes.

title: Protected GET /api/me/games — list authenticated user's own games (paginated)
description: Fourth feature of the auth bundle. Adds GET /api/me/games (requires Bearer JWT) that returns the authenticated user's games, paginated. The Game flow is extended: when a Game is created via POST /api/games AND the request is authenticated, the active-state Player on the corresponding side carries the user id, and at archive time the games row gets white_user_id and/or black_user_id populated; when the request is anonymous (no Authorization header), both columns stay null and the existing X-Player-Id flow continues unchanged. Query is a direct filter on games (no join through an intermediate players table — see feature 16 for the data-model decision). This wires up the actual product benefit the user stated: 'con una cuenta se pueden revisar las partidas jugadas'.
acceptance: GET /api/me/games?page={p}&size={s} requires authentication; without Bearer JWT returns 401.
Default pagination size 20, max 100. Response includes total count and page metadata.

Game creation flow: GameService.createGame is extended to accept an optional currentUserId; if present, the active-state Player on the corresponding side carries the user id, and the archive path writes it to games.white_user_id or games.black_user_id (whichever side maps to the authenticated user).
Anonymous flow regression: existing POST /api/games without Authorization still works and produces games rows with white_user_id = black_user_id = NULL. Existing IT must stay green.

Integration test MyGamesIT covers: (a) 401 without auth; (b) authenticated user sees only their own games; (c) games created anonymously are NOT visible to any authenticated user (fresh-start identity model confirmed); (d) pagination boundaries.

MyGamesQuery service or a JPA query method on GameRepository runs the direct filter `WHERE white_user_id = :userId OR black_user_id = :userId ORDER BY ended_at DESC` and projects only the fields needed for the list response (id, opponent display name as denormalised on games, status, result, ended_at).
Feature note notes/19-auth-my-games.md.
README.md API section documents the new endpoint.
docs/architecture.md API-contract section adds the endpoint.
./init.sh passes.

title: WebSocket auth — JWT-validated STOMP CONNECT + ChannelInterceptor identity check
description: Fifth and final feature of the auth bundle. Closes the gap left after features 16–19: REST is auth-aware, but the WebSocket / STOMP surface still trusts whatever playerId or X-Player-Id the client sends. This feature adds a STOMP ChannelInterceptor that (a) on CONNECT, reads the Authorization header from the native STOMP headers and, if present, validates the JWT against the same JwtVerifier used by REST; (b) on SEND/SUBSCRIBE, verifies that the playerId in the frame corresponds to either the authenticated user's player rows OR a matching X-Player-Id anonymous session. Anonymous WebSocket use is preserved for guest play, but identity spoofing is blocked.
acceptance: WebSocketConfig registers a ChannelInterceptor at io.github.dariogguillen.chess.websocket.StompAuthInterceptor.
On STOMP CONNECT: if Authorization header is present and valid, the session is tagged with the user id; if absent, the session is anonymous (current behaviour preserved).

On STOMP SEND/SUBSCRIBE that carries a playerId: the interceptor verifies the playerId belongs to either (a) the authenticated user's known Player rows, or (b) the anonymous session's known X-Player-Id (whichever was used to create the player). Mismatch returns an ERROR frame and disconnects.

Integration test StompAuthIT covers: (a) STOMP connect+subscribe+send anonymous still works (regression); (b) STOMP connect with valid JWT works and the session is identified; (c) STOMP connect with invalid/expired JWT works but session stays anonymous (does NOT reject the connection — only blocks identity spoofing); (d) SEND from an authenticated session with a playerId that belongs to a different user returns an ERROR; (e) SEND from an anonymous session with a playerId not matching X-Player-Id returns an ERROR.
Feature note notes/20-auth-stomp-trust.md with explicit cross-ecosystem comparison (the http4s+fs2 WebSocket auth idiom).
docs/architecture.md WebSocket section documents the trust model.
README.md WebSocket subsection mentions the JWT auth option.
./init.sh passes.

title: Let the room creator choose their side (WHITE/BLACK/RANDOM)
description: Today RoomService.createRoom assigns the creator to white by convention; the joiner takes black (no way to override at room creation time). The frontend's 'Configure Room' view shows a WHITE/BLACK toggle but the backend ignores it. This feature wires that toggle through: CreateRoomRequest gains an optional Side preference, RoomService.createRoom honours it, the joiner gets the opposite side automatically. RANDOM uses a server-side coin flip (anti-cheat: clients cannot bias the result). Backwards-compatible: null/missing field defaults to WHITE so existing frontend without the field works unchanged.
acceptance: CreateRoomRequest record gains a nullable Side preferredSide field (null → WHITE for backwards compatibility) with allowable values WHITE, BLACK, RANDOM.
RoomService.createRoom signature accepts the preferredSide parameter; WHITE → creator is white; BLACK → creator is black; RANDOM → backend coin flip via SecureRandom or ThreadLocalRandom.
Joiner receives the opposite side automatically (existing behaviour preserved when the creator chose WHITE).

Integration test covers: explicit WHITE preference, explicit BLACK preference, RANDOM preference asserts both sides are reachable over multiple invocations (or asserts the field populates either side via a seeded random), null preference yields WHITE (regression for the existing frontend version).
Springdoc schema documents the new field with @Schema(allowableValues = {"WHITE", "BLACK", "RANDOM"}) so the typed client codegen produces a literal union.
Frontend coordination: the existing WHITE/BLACK toggle in chess-frontend's Configure Room view maps to this field. Cross-repo additive change — old frontends keep working without the field.
docs/architecture.md API contract section documents the new field.
./init.sh passes.

title: Server-authoritative clock (time control + auto-flagging)
description: Introduce per-player remaining-time tracking on the active Game state, server-side flag detection (when a player runs out of time, the game terminates automatically), and STOMP broadcasts of clock state on every move and termination event. The backend is the canonical clock source — the frontend renders it. Four reasons this MUST live in the backend: (1) anti-cheating — clients cannot lie about remaining time; (2) source of truth — both players and spectators see the same synchronised clock; (3) auto-flagging — when a side hits zero the game terminates authoritatively even if the flagged player is offline; (4) disconnect/reconnect — the clock keeps running while the client is down and the reconnect reads canonical state. MVP supports a single declared time control per room (e.g. 5 minutes per side, no increment); the request shape allows future formats without an API break.
acceptance: Active Game state (Redis) gains whiteTimeRemainingMs, blackTimeRemainingMs, and lastMoveAt (Instant); fields are null when the room has no time control declared (backwards compatibility with the no-clock flow).
CreateRoomRequest gains an optional TimeControl record (e.g. { initialMs, incrementMs }); null = no clock (existing untimed behaviour).
On each successful move, the moving player's remaining time decreases by (now - lastMoveAt), and lastMoveAt is updated to now. The first move's elapsed time counts against the white player.

Scheduled task (Spring @Scheduled at e.g. 1s tick, OR a per-game timer) checks whether the side-to-move has run out: when their projected remaining time hits 0 with the game still active, terminate with status TIMEOUT and broadcast a TerminationEvent.
MoveEvent payload extended with whiteTimeRemainingMs and blackTimeRemainingMs so the frontend renders a synchronised clock without polling.
GameStateResponse exposes the same remaining-time fields so a reconnecting client sees the canonical clock state.

Integration test covers: clock initialised to TimeControl.initialMs, decrement on move, MoveEvent carries clock state, scheduled flag-detection terminates a game even with no further moves, untimed rooms (TimeControl = null) bypass the clock surface entirely (regression).
docs/architecture.md documents the clock as server-authoritative; mentions disconnect-resilience invariant and the scheduled-flag-detection model.
Cross-repo: frontend adds a clock UI; additive (older frontends without TimeControl still create untimed rooms).
./init.sh passes.

title: Play against a bot (Stockfish via subprocess)
description: Add a 'Play against the bot' room creation path. When the creator selects BOT as the opponent kind, the backend creates a Game with the creator as one side and a bot (Stockfish) as the other and, on the bot's turn, invokes a chess engine to compute and play a move via the same move-validation + broadcast pipeline a human move uses. Recommendation: Stockfish bundled into the Docker image and driven via subprocess + UCI protocol. Single difficulty in MVP (e.g. skill level 5 or depth 10); difficulty selection is a follow-up. Selected over Java-only minimax because portfolio signal is significantly higher (Stockfish integration is a real-world recruiter signal) and the bot is actually strong enough to be interesting.
acceptance: CreateRoomRequest gains an opponentKind field: FRIEND | BOT | RANDOM (null = FRIEND for backwards compatibility). Today only FRIEND exists implicitly.

When BOT is chosen, RoomService creates a complete two-side Game immediately (no waiting on a joiner). The bot side carries a fixed display name (e.g. 'Stockfish') and a sentinel Player.userId pattern that future features can recognise.
Dockerfile updated to install the Stockfish binary (multi-stage build or Alpine package). The image size cost and the deploy.yml impact are documented in the feature note.

BotEngineService at io.github.dariogguillen.chess.service.bot wraps the subprocess: lifecycle (start per game / pool / start-on-demand — implementer's call documented), sends UCI position + go commands, parses bestmove response, handles process death.

After a human move is validated, if the opponent is the bot, the bot's move is computed (synchronously or on a separate executor) and broadcast as a MoveEvent indistinguishable on the wire from a two-human-game move.
Engine timeout / process death is handled gracefully: log + abandon the game with a structured TerminationEvent reason BOT_ENGINE_FAILURE; the JVM does not deadlock or leak processes.
Single difficulty in MVP. Difficulty selection is explicitly out-of-scope and tracked as a follow-up.

Integration test covers: a vs-bot game completes a known short forced-mate sequence, bot moves are legal, engine process is reaped on game termination. (Testcontainers or Maven-managed Stockfish binary on CI.)
docs/architecture.md documents the bot integration (UCI protocol, subprocess lifecycle, deploy implications).
./init.sh passes (Stockfish must be available to the test runner; the IT either spawns the production binary or uses a pinned test binary).

title: Play against a random opponent (online matchmaking queue)
description: Add a 'Play random' room creation path. The creator joins a Redis-backed waiting queue; when another player also requests random, they are matched into a room together. Players in the queue see a 'waiting for opponent' state via STOMP; when matched, the existing room/game flow takes over. MVP uses a single global queue; once feature 22 (time-control) lands, the queue partitions by declared time control. Anonymous and authenticated players both eligible — matchmaking does NOT gate on auth.
acceptance: CreateRoomRequest.opponentKind = RANDOM enqueues the player rather than creating a room immediately. The endpoint returns a queue-ticket response (e.g. a queue id + a STOMP topic to subscribe to for the match notification).
Redis-backed queue: single global key in MVP (e.g. a Redis list or sorted set under random-queue:*). Operation is atomic — two simultaneous requests cannot both match the same waiting player.

When the queue has two players, the backend creates a room with both, assigns sides (random coin flip OR creator-first WHITE — implementer's call, documented in the feature note), and notifies BOTH players via a STOMP RoomMatchedEvent (or the existing RoomJoinedEvent shape, additive).

Abandonment handling: if a queued player disconnects, explicitly cancels, or times out after N seconds (e.g. 60s), they are removed from the queue cleanly. Leaked queue entries would break future matches.
Concurrency: two simultaneous queue-joins from the same player do not double-enqueue.

Integration test covers: two players join the queue → matched into the same room, abandonment removes the entry, simultaneous joins do not double-match, anonymous (X-Player-Id) and authenticated (JWT) eligibility.
Once feature 22 (time-control) is shipped, the queue partitions by TimeControl (a 5+0 player only matches another 5+0 player). MVP can ship without partitioning.
docs/architecture.md documents the queue model, abandonment policy, and the time-control partition consideration.
./init.sh passes.

title: Hotfix: auth env vars in docker-compose + CorsConfigurationSource bean for Spring Security 6 filter chain
description: Retroactive entry for a same-day production hotfix on 2026-05-29. After the auth bundle (16-20) deployed, three latent issues surfaced together and broke production cross-origin requests from the Cloudflare Pages frontend. (1) docker-compose.prod.yml's `environment:` block declared each env var explicitly and never listed the four auth-bundle vars (AUTH_JWT_SECRET, AUTH_OAUTH_FRONTEND_REDIRECT_BASE, GOOGLE_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID, GOOGLE_OAUTH_CLIENT_SECRET) — docker compose reads .env for variable substitution in the yml but does NOT pass .env entries to the container by default, so Spring saw `auth.jwt.secret` as blank and AuthProperties.Jwt's compact constructor failed boot fast. (2) CorsConfig.java relied on WebMvcConfigurer.addCorsMappings, which operates at the DispatcherServlet level — AFTER the security filter chain. Feature 16's SecurityConfig.cors(Customizer.withDefaults()) requires a CorsConfigurationSource bean to handle preflights at the filter-chain level; without it, OPTIONS preflights returned 200 but with no CORS headers, browsers blocked the cross-origin POST. (3) An initial fix attempt for (1) included `CHESS_CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGIN_PATTERNS: ${CHESS_CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGIN_PATTERNS:-}` — the empty-default substitution passed an empty string to the container, overriding application.yml's correct production default (https://chess-frontend-52i.pages.dev,http://localhost:*), tripping the CorsProperties compact constructor. The fix removed the line entirely; if a future operator wants to override CORS at runtime they wire `${CHESS_CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGIN_PATTERNS}` (no `:-` default) and set the value in .env. Skipped the full harness cycle because the bugs were independently obvious and production was broken — leader applied edits directly, ./init.sh verified green locally, user committed and deployed. Documented retroactively here and in progress/history.md to preserve the rastro the harness usually provides.
acceptance: docker-compose.prod.yml `environment:` block on the app service now passes AUTH_JWT_SECRET, AUTH_OAUTH_FRONTEND_REDIRECT_BASE, GOOGLE_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID, GOOGLE_OAUTH_CLIENT_SECRET to the container via `${VAR}` substitution from /opt/chess/.env.

CorsConfig.java exposes a CorsConfigurationSource bean built from the same CorsProperties as the WebMvcConfigurer.addCorsMappings path, so Spring Security's filter-chain CORS handling and the dispatcher-level CORS handling stay in sync (single source of truth preserved from feature 10).
CHESS_CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGIN_PATTERNS is deliberately NOT in the docker-compose.prod.yml environment block; application.yml's production default applies unless an operator wires it explicitly.

Post-deploy smoke test: OPTIONS preflight to /api/rooms with Origin https://chess-frontend-52i.pages.dev returns 200 with access-control-allow-origin matching the origin; frontend successfully creates a room via cross-origin POST.
./init.sh passes (verified before user push).
Documented in progress/history.md as a retroactive entry with the operational lesson about docker-compose.prod.yml drift between repo and EC2 (the immediate predecessor of feature 26).

title: Sync docker-compose.prod.yml to EC2 on every deploy (eliminate manual drift)
description: Operational hardening surfaced by feature 25. The deploy.yml workflow currently does `docker compose pull && up -d` against the docker-compose.prod.yml that already lives on the EC2 host. The yml on EC2 is independent of the yml in the repo — any edit to the repo's yml does NOT reach production. Feature 25's same-day hotfix required manual SSH edits to /opt/chess/docker-compose.prod.yml because of this; without that, the env-var fix in the repo would have shipped to a host that ignored it. Solution: add an `scp` step (or rsync with --checksum) to deploy.yml that copies the repo's docker-compose.prod.yml to /opt/chess/ on the EC2 host BEFORE `docker compose pull && up -d` runs. The /opt/chess/.env stays manually managed on EC2 (credentials must not be in the repo); only the yml syncs.
acceptance: deploy.yml gains an `scp` (or equivalent) step that copies docker-compose.prod.yml from the workflow runner to /opt/chess/docker-compose.prod.yml on EC2, preserving the existing file mode and owner.
The new step runs BEFORE the `docker compose pull && up -d` step so the new yml is the one compose reads.
The /opt/chess/.env file is NEVER touched by the workflow — it stays operator-managed on the EC2 host (credentials live there).
If the yml has not changed since the last deploy, the sync is a no-op (or at worst a redundant write — acceptable).
After this feature ships, a future change to docker-compose.prod.yml in the repo lands on EC2 with the next push to main; no manual SSH edit required.
Smoke test: edit docker-compose.prod.yml (e.g. add a harmless comment), push, confirm the file on EC2 reflects the change after the deploy completes.
docs/architecture.md and docs/deploy-runbook.md document the yml-sync behaviour and the env-file's continued operator-managed status.
./init.sh passes (no application-code changes expected).

title: Spectators join the room, not the game (viewer count from the waiting lobby)
description: Today spectators are game-keyed: feature 6.5's ViewerCountTracker counts STOMP sessions subscribed to /topic/games/{gameId} and publishes to /topic/games/{gameId}/viewers. But the gameId does not exist until the second player joins (RoomService.joinRoom creates the Game), so a creator who wants friends watching while waiting for an opponent has nothing for them to subscribe to. This feature re-keys spectators to the ROOM: the viewer count is tracked over /topic/rooms/{roomId} and published to /topic/rooms/{roomId}/viewers, so it works from WAITING_FOR_PLAYER through ACTIVE and stays stable across the transition (no reset, no double-count). The game state (MoveEvent, etc.) stays on /topic/games/{gameId}; spectators transition to it on RoomJoinedEvent (feature 9.5) for the live board. Decision taken with the user: unified room-keyed count (not a dual game+room count); the game-keyed /viewers topic from feature 6.5 is retired. The frontend has no viewer-count UI yet, so this is a free contract change. Access control (a separate join token so spectators sharing the roomId cannot occupy the player slot) is deliberately split into feature room-access-tokens (priority 22.7); this feature does NOT change the join contract. Direct invitations to registered users are a later feature.
acceptance: ViewerCountTracker re-keyed from game to room: it counts STOMP sessions subscribed to /topic/rooms/{roomId} (regex anchored so /topic/rooms/{roomId}/viewers does not self-count) that are NOT players of the room, and publishes ViewerCountEvent to /topic/rooms/{roomId}/viewers on every change.
The count works in WAITING_FOR_PLAYER (no game yet): a spectator can subscribe and be counted BEFORE the opponent joins.
The count is stable across the WAITING_FOR_PLAYER -> ACTIVE transition: a spectator subscribed to the room stays counted when the game starts (no reset, no duplication).

Player exclusion is based on room.players() via the trusted playerId native STOMP header (same trust-on-declaration model as feature 6.5; a player subscribing to /topic/rooms/{roomId} sends its playerId to self-exclude). RoomService.findById is the lookup; a subscribe to a non-existent room counts the session as a viewer (tolerant, mirrors the old game behaviour).
ViewerCountEvent changes shape from (UUID gameId, int count) to (String roomId, int count); roomId is the 6-char short code, not a UUID.

The game-keyed viewer count (/topic/games/{gameId}/viewers) from feature 6.5 is retired; the tracker no longer listens to /topic/games/* . Game state (MoveEvent and the GameStateEvent family) stays on /topic/games/{gameId} unchanged.
Decrement on SessionUnsubscribeEvent and SessionDisconnectEvent preserved (the last-subscription-of-session check and disconnect cleanup carry over to the room keying).

No new REST endpoint: the spectator flow reuses GET /api/rooms/{id} (feature 9.5) for the initial snapshot and /topic/rooms/{roomId} for live lifecycle + count; on RoomJoinedEvent the spectator subscribes to /topic/games/{gameId} for moves. Documented in the feature note + architecture doc.

ViewerCountIT migrated to room keying and extended: spectator counted in WAITING_FOR_PLAYER (the headline case); count stable across the opponent's join; room creator subscribing with its playerId is NOT counted; two spectators -> 2; disconnect and unsubscribe tick the count down. Existing RoomLifecycleIT stays green.

docs/architecture.md spectator section updated to the room-keyed model: the new topic, the retirement of the game-keyed /viewers topic, player exclusion via room.players(), and the spectator subscribe/transition flow.
Feature note notes/22.5-spectators-in-room.md follows the template; Scala/Typelevel parallels included.
README out of scope (no run-procedure change); the reviewer confirms README is unchanged.

Cross-repo: STOMP contract change (viewers topic game -> room; ViewerCountEvent shape) is safe because the frontend has no viewer-count consumer yet; the frontend implements the room-keyed model directly. Players subscribing to /topic/rooms/{roomId} must send their playerId header to self-exclude (coordination note).
./init.sh passes.

title: Separate join token so a shared room link cannot be used to grab the player slot
description: Surfaced while planning spectators-in-room. Today the roomId is the single shared secret: POST /api/rooms/{id}/join only needs the roomId, so anyone the creator shares the room with (e.g. spectators) could occupy the second-player slot. This feature separates the two capabilities: a secret joinToken (only the creator receives it, required by the join endpoint) vs the roomId (used to watch: GET /api/rooms/{id} + SUBSCRIBE /topic/rooms/{id}). The creator builds two links in the frontend: a 'play' link carrying the joinToken for the opponent, and a 'watch' link with just the roomId for friends. Whoever has only the watch link cannot join as a player. Direct invitations to registered users (now that accounts exist) are a separate later feature layered on top of this. Builds on spectators-in-room (22.5).
acceptance: POST /api/rooms generates a secret joinToken in addition to the roomId; the create response returns it (only the creator obtains it).

POST /api/rooms/{id}/join requires a valid joinToken (header or body, decided at plan time); a missing or wrong token returns 403 (a new error code in the GlobalExceptionHandler + ErrorResponse enum).
The roomId remains the identifier for watching (GET /api/rooms/{id}, SUBSCRIBE /topic/rooms/{id}) and does NOT by itself authorise joining as a player.

Frontend coordination strategy documented: the production join flow today sends no token, so the plan states the migration approach (coordinated deploy vs temporary optional-token fallback) explicitly.
Springdoc + docs/architecture.md API contract updated for the new join requirement and the create-response field.
Integration tests: join with valid token succeeds; join with missing/invalid token returns 403; the watch path (GET + room subscribe) needs no token.
Feature note notes/22.7-room-access-tokens.md.
./init.sh passes.

title: Choose the bot's strength by Elo (Stockfish UCI_LimitStrength)
description: Follow-up to bot-opponent (23): today the bot plays at a single fixed strength (chess.bot.move-time, no Skill Level / Elo limit). This feature lets the room creator pick the bot's strength as a target Elo when creating a BOT room. Decision taken with the user: Elo (not 3 named tiers) because discrete tiers jump too coarsely. The backend uses Stockfish's UCI_LimitStrength + UCI_Elo. Stockfish's UCI_Elo floor is ~1320 (it clamps internally if its build's range is narrower), so the offered range is ~1320-3190; sub-1320 beginner play via Skill Level is a later follow-up. The chosen Elo is threaded CreateRoomRequest -> the BOT Game (a new nullable field, same pattern as timeControl) and applied on every bot move. Persisting the Elo to Postgres history is out of scope (no migration); only the Redis active Game carries it.
acceptance: CreateRoomRequest gains a nullable Integer botElo (relevant only when opponentKind=BOT) with @Min/@Max bounding it to the supported Stockfish UCI_Elo range (~1320-3190) and a @Schema documenting it; null/omitted -> a configured default (chess.bot.default-elo). An out-of-range value returns 400 VALIDATION_FAILED (Bean Validation; no new error code).

Game gains a nullable Integer botElo, threaded from createRoom's BOT path into the Game, using the same backwards-compatible record-evolution pattern as timeControl (convenience constructors delegate; compact constructor tolerates null; withStatus/withClock carry it through); null for non-bot games.

BotEngine.chooseMove signature carries the Elo; StockfishBotEngine issues setoption name UCI_LimitStrength value true + setoption name UCI_Elo value <elo> before the search. Stockfish clamps internally if asked for a value outside its build's range. The deterministic test double / Mockito bean ignores the Elo (or is verified to receive it).
BotProperties gains default-elo (validated within the supported range); BotMoveService passes game.botElo() (or the default) to the engine on every bot move.
Anonymous/FRIEND regression: non-bot games are unaffected (botElo null, no UCI_Elo option); a BOT room created without botElo uses the default and still plays.

Integration tests: botElo threaded into the Game and passed to the engine (verify on the mocked BotEngine); out-of-range botElo -> 400; null botElo on a BOT room -> default applied; the gated StockfishEngineIT updated to the new signature and (when the binary is present) confirms the UCI_LimitStrength/UCI_Elo option exchange does not break move selection.
Springdoc @Schema documents botElo + its range; docs/architecture.md bot section documents the Elo-limited strength model and the ~1320 floor caveat.
Persisting the Elo to Postgres history is explicitly out of scope (no Flyway migration this feature); stated in the plan + note.

./init.sh passes.

title: Lichess-style bot strength via Fairy-Stockfish (Skill Level + depth, sub-1320 Elo)
description: Follow-up to bot-difficulty (23.5). Official Stockfish's UCI_Elo floors at ~1320, so it cannot play like a beginner. This feature adopts Lichess's model: switch the engine to Fairy-Stockfish (Skill Level range -20..20, the negatives are what reach <1320) and replace the UCI_LimitStrength/UCI_Elo strength model with a mapping from the requested Elo to (Skill Level, search depth), interpolating Lichess's published 8-level table (lila modules/fishnet AiPerf.scala / the play-with-computer config). The public interface stays Elo (CreateRoomRequest.botElo, Game.botElo) — only the internal mapping + UCI options change. The allowed Elo range is widened downward to ~400. Phase 1 deliberately does NOT implement Lichess's MultiPV-4 + randomized-weakness layer (Fairy's native Skill Level already randomizes); that is a documented Phase 2 follow-up. Decision taken with the user: follow Lichess; Elo interface kept; Fairy-Stockfish; no MultiPV in Phase 1.
acceptance: Engine switched to Fairy-Stockfish: the Dockerfile installs the Fairy-Stockfish binary (from its GitHub releases for linux x86-64; not in apt), and chess.bot.engine-path default is updated to the installed path. Image-size impact documented in the note.

The UCI_Elo strength model from 23.5 is replaced by the Lichess model: a mapping from the requested Elo to (Skill Level, search depth) interpolating Lichess's level table (Skill Level -20..20, negative for sub-~1320 Elo; depth caps per tier). StockfishBotEngine issues setoption name Skill Level value <s> and searches with go depth <d> (per the mapped tier) instead of UCI_LimitStrength/UCI_Elo.

The Elo->(skill,depth) mapping is verified against lila's current config (not just the forum summary) and documented in the feature note with the source link; the mapping lives in one place (a small pure function / table) that is unit-tested at the tier boundaries and for interpolation.

Allowed Elo range widened downward (e.g. MIN_BOT_ELO ~400, MAX_BOT_ELO ~3190); the single-source-of-truth constants referenced by the DTO @Min/@Max and BotProperties.defaultElo are updated together; default-elo stays valid.

Public interface unchanged: CreateRoomRequest.botElo and Game.botElo keep their shape and threading; only the internal mapping + emitted UCI options change. Out-of-range still -> 400 VALIDATION_FAILED (no new error code).
MultiPV-4 + randomized weakness is explicitly OUT OF SCOPE (Phase 2 follow-up); the note documents why Fairy's native Skill Level is enough for Phase 1 and what Phase 2 would add.

Tests: the Elo->(skill,depth) mapping unit test (tier boundaries, interpolation, the new low floor); the engine wiring (Skill Level + go depth emitted, NOT UCI_Elo); validation of the widened range (below new MIN / above MAX -> 400, null -> default); the gated engine IT updated to Fairy-Stockfish (assumeTrue the fairy binary on PATH; skips cleanly when absent). The deterministic test double keeps ./init.sh binary-free and green.
docs/architecture.md updated: the Lichess-derived strength model, the Fairy-Stockfish dependency and why (sub-1320 floor), the skill/depth mapping, and the MultiPV Phase-2 deferral.
